meta.title: Japan Cultural & Business Knowledge
meta.disclaimer: General cultural guidance for Ugandan partners preparing to work with Japanese investors.

facts: Bowing is a standard greeting; depth reflects respect level.
Business cards (meishi) are exchanged with both hands and a slight bow — treat the card respectfully, do not write on it in front of the giver.
Use family name + san (e.g., Tanaka-san) until invited to use given names.
A quiet, respectful tone is valued in first meetings.
Punctuality is highly valued — arriving 5–10 minutes early is normal.
Hierarchy matters: senior person often speaks first or sits in the place of honour.
Meetings follow structure: agenda, detailed materials, and careful note-taking.
Silence often means thoughtful consideration, not necessarily disagreement.
Decisions may take time due to internal consensus (nemawashi) — patience is expected.
'Hai' (yes) often means 'I hear you' rather than 'I agree' — confirm understanding explicitly.
Written proposals with detail and data are respected.
Long decision cycles reflect internal alignment, not lack of interest.
Avoid putting a Japanese partner on the spot for an immediate public decision.
Communication can be indirect; read context and tone, not only words.
Preserving harmony (wa) is important — direct confrontation in groups is often avoided.
Written follow-up emails summarizing agreed points are appreciated.
Gift-giving is common but follows etiquette — modest, well-wrapped gifts are appropriate.
Prepare detailed written materials — Japanese companies often expect thorough documentation.
Respect punctuality and send materials in advance when possible.
Understand that a slow response may mean internal review, not rejection.
Gifts from Uganda (coffee, crafts) can be meaningful if presented modestly and with context.
Explain Ugandan relationship-building norms gently — many Japanese investors are learning too.
